turns.text: If I understand you correctly, the Defendant Von Schirach concerned himself only with such matters as were reported to him by the Regierungsprasident or which were brought to his special attention in writing; is that true?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Yes, that is true.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Witness, were you yourself a member of the SS?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, I was never a member of the SS.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Of the SA?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Do you know that these three permanent representatives, whom the Defendant Von Schirach had in Vienna, namely the Regierungsprasident, the Deputy Gauleiter, and the Mayor, were all three SS Fuehrer?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Yes, I know that.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: How was that? Did the Defendant Von Schirach select these men himself, or how do you explain the fact that all three of his representatives were SS Fuehrer?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: The Deputy Gauleiter, Scharizer, was an honorary SS Fuehrer and, as far as I recall, he was Oberbefehlsleiter of the Party. When Von Schirach came to Vienna, Scharizer had already been active for several years in Vienna.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: As what?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: As Deputy Gauleiter. I do not know when the Regierungsprasident, Dr. Dellbrugge, came to Vienna; but I assume either before or at about the same time as Von Schirach. Moreover, the Regierungsprasidenten were appointed by the Ministry of the Interior, so that I think he could hardly have had sufficient influence to refuse or select a particular Regierungsprasident. As for the mayor, the situation was similar.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: He was a certain Blaschke?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Yes. He was SS Brigadefuehrer Blaschke, he was also appointed by the Ministry of the Interior as acting mayor.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: By the Ministry of the Interior?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: When was that?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I believe that was in 1944, in January or February of 1944.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Do you know that this SS Brigadefuehrer, or whatever he was, this Blaschke, before the time of the Defendant Von Schirach, was active in Vienna as a town councillor, and I believe also as vice mayor?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: He was a town councillor before; and I believe he was vice mayor before I came to Vienna.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Do you know that the Defendant Von Schirach for a long time opposed this SS Oberfuehrer or Brigadefuehrer Blaschke being appointed mayor of Vienna?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I should say he opposed this for about 6 or 9 months, and I believe later he refused to allow the Minister of the Interior finally to confirm his appointment as mayor.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Witness, what were the relations between the Defendant Von Schirach and the SS and the SS officers? Were they especially friendly and cordial or what were they like?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: As far as I know, Schirach associated with the SS Fuehrer as far as was officially necessary and no more.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Was he friendly with SS men?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No; I do not know. In any case I knew of no such friendship.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did he not express to you his attitude toward the SS?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I have already said that he always had the feeling that he was under a certain supervision from them and for that reason he was rather distrustful.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Distrustful of . ..

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Of the SS.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Witness, do you know how the Defendant Von Schirach received his information about the foreign press and foreign press reports?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: He received them from the Reich Propaganda Office in Vienna. They were excerpts which the Propaganda Ministry issued in collaboration with the Reich Press Chief, Dr. Dietrich. As far, as I know, however, they were selected and screened.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did you live for a long time with Von Schirach in Vienna?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: From 1944 on I lived in Schirach's house.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: You also took your meals with him?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Yes, I also took meals with him.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did not the Defendant Von Schirach obtain information from the foreign radio?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, I am almost certain he did not, because after every meal he listened to the official German news services with me and a few other co-workers. Besides, if he had done so it would in my opinion have become known very soon for, as I said already, he had the feeling that he was being watched.

turns.speaker: TEE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Sauter, the witness can only tell us what he knows. How could he know whether Von Schirach ever listened to any foreign news? If he does not know, why do you not take him on to something else?

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: The witness said, Mr. President, that during the latter part of his time in Vienna, from the spring of 1944 I believe he said, he lived in the house of the Defendant Von Schirach.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes, I know he said that, and he said that he did not think he heard foreign news. What more can he give? What more evidence can he give on that subject?

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: I wanted to hear that, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: But he said it already. I have taken it down. Thy do you not go on to something else?

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Witness, do you know that in the last weeks of the resistance an order came to Vienna from Berlin according to which all defeatists, whether men or women, were to be hanged? What attitude did Schirach take toward this order?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I know that so-called courts martial were to be set up with the purpose of speedily sentencing people who objected to the conduct of the war or who showed themselves to be defeatists. This court martial was set up in Vienna, or rather appointed, but it did not meet once, and thus did not pronounce any sentences.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did the court-martial of the Defendant Von Schirach carry on any proceedings at all?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, not to my knowledge.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Do you know anything about it?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Sauter, that fact, again, was given in evidence by Von Schirach and was not cross-examined to-that that court martial did not meet.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Witness, do you know anything about the fact that in the last weeks an order came to form franc-tireur units? What was Von Schirach's attitude to that?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I do not know that franc-tireur units were to be formed, but I do know that a "Freikorps Hitler" was to be formed. They were to be in civilian clothes. Schirach ordered that no people from the Reichsgau Vienna were to be assigned to this "Freikorps."

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Why not?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Because at that time he considered resistance senseless. Secondly, because he considered it contrary to international law.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: My last question to you, Witness. You were with Schirach to the last, until he left Henna?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did Schirach give an order to destroy bridges or churches, residential quarters, and so form, in Vienna?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, I do not know of that.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: What was the position he took?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: That orders to blow up bridges or to take any defense measures were given only by the military authorities, as far as I know.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: But not by Schirach?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Mr. President, I have no more questions to put to this witness.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Does any other defendant's counsel want to ask questions? The Prosecution?

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Witness, would you see all of the files that were in Von Schirach's office during the time that you were his adjutant?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I have already told you, or I told the defense counsel, that most of the mail went through the Central Bureau.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: I want to show you a document that is in evidence here and ask you if you can tell us whether or not you have seen this before. [A document was handed to the witness.] Have you ever seen that document before?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I do not know this document officially, as I see it is dated 28 May 1942, at which time I was an officer in the Luftwaffe.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: I see, you did not mean the Tribunal to understand that you were familiar with everything that was in Von Schirach's files, because certainly this document was there during the years that you were his adjutant. You never saw it. It is marked "Central Bureau," and you had charge of these very files, yet you never saw this teletype to Bormann? So you certainly did not know everything that was in his files, did you?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I said that the majority of the mail went through my offices but, of course, since I was not in Vienna at this time but only came to Vienna in April 1943, I was not able to look through all the back documents and letters in the files of the Reich Governor. That would have taken years.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Let me ask you something else. You were there in the last days, I assume, when the city was taken by the Allied Forces, were you not?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I was in Vienna until April 1945.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: What was done with Von Schirach's files when the end was very obviously coming? What did you do with all those files over which you had control?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I was not in charge of any files. I was chief of the bureau, and I,..

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Well, you know what I mean chief of the bureau or of the office where these files were kept. What I want to know is what did you do with the files?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I gave no orders in this connection.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Do you know what became of the files?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, I do not.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: They were taken out of the of lice sometime before the city was captured; do you not know that?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, I did not know that.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Were the files there the last day that you were there?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Probably, yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: I do not want a "probably." I want to know if you know and if you do, to tell us. Were they there or not the last day that you were in the office?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I gave no orders to destroy them or to remove them.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: I did not ask you if you gave orders. I asked you if you know what became of them and whether or not they were in the of lice the last day that you were there?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I do not know what happened to them. Nor can I say whether they were still there on the last day.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Do you not know that they were all moved to a salt mine in Austria?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, I do not know that.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: You have never heard that, or that they were taken out of the office and were later found by the Allied Forces in a salt mine?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, I do not know that.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: I do not mean that you heard they were found there, but you certainly knew that they were taken out of the of lice?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, I do not know. I also gave no orders.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Well, now, let me put this proposition to you, and then perhaps you can give an explanation of it to the Tribunal. This document that I have just shown to you and these reports that you examined for Dr. Sauter were all found in Schirach's files in a salt mine. Would you have any explanation for that?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, I cannot explain that.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: They were found together. Would that mean anything to you, or would you have any explanation for it?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, I have not. I can only explain that by saying that probably the Chief of the Reich Governor's office or one of his officials who was in charge of these things gave the order to that effect, of course without my knowledge and without any order from me.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Tell the Tribunal exactly what day you closed up your office in Vienna, or the last day that you were in this office.

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: It might have been the 3rd or 4th of April.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: When was the city taken?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I read in the newspaper afterwards that the city finally fell into the hands of the Allies on 13 April

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Did you all leave your office on the ad or 4th of April? Did Von Schirach leave as well, and all the clerical staff, et cetera?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Schirach and I and his adjutant left the office on this day, or rather, Schirach had previously set up his of lice at his home and was working there.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Had he taken any files from his of lice to his home?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Only what he needed immediately to carry on his business; that is, the matters which were being dealt with at the moment.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Did you leave someone in charge of the files when you left there, you and Von Schirach- on the ad of April; and if you did, who was it that you left in charge?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I did not leave anyone to supervise. The file clerks did that of their own accord.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: I am trying to understand-and I think it would be helpful to the Tribunal-whether or not you just walked out of this of lice and left everything there, or whether just you and Von Schirach left and left other people there, or whether the place was in such chaos that nobody remained. I have not any accurate picture of it, and I think it is of some importance. You ought to be able to tell us. You left there with him. What was the situation on the ad or 4th of April? The city was practically to be taken in another 10 days. It was under siege. There was much confusion. What were you doing about your files and all of your other papers in your office when you walked out of there that day? You certainly just did not walk out and not give some directions.

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I believe that we are not clear about the character of the Central Bureau. The Central Bureau, of which I was in charge for the last few months, had no powers, no executive powers, but all of these things were done by the competent Reich Governor, that is, the Regierungsprasident, and he probably...

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: I do not need any explanation of how your office was set up. I want to know if the papers were left there or not, or if anybody was left with them.

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: The papers, as far as I know, were left there, and the archivists were instructed to take care of them.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Did you order any papers to be destroyed before you walked out that day, the ad or 4th of April, anything at all?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: I gave no orders to destroy anything in the Reich Governor's Office; I had no authority to do that.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Did anybody to your knowledge order anything destroyed, whether you did or not?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Whether such an order was given and who gave it, I do not know.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: I have no further questions.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: What is the document you put to him?

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Number USA-865. It is Document 3877-PS, a teletype to Bormann from Von Schirach on 28 May 1942.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do you want to re-examine the witness, Dr. Sauter?

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Witness, I should like to go back to what the Prosecution just asked you. The documents of the Reich Governor's office apparently are supposed to have been found in a salt mine. Did you have any supervision over the documents of the Reich Governor's office?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: No, I had no supervision over these documents. I just explained that. For that reason, I could not give any order to remove them. I know that valuable objects, pictures, and so on, were removed, but much earlier.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: And the other employees of the Central Bureau, were they Viennese? Did they stay in the office, or what do you know about that?

turns.speaker: HOEPKEN
turns.text: Most of them were Viennese, of course, and probably remained behind. I shook hands and said goodbye to them, and then we separated. I also asked whether I could do anything for them, and then I left Vienna.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: I have no more questions, Mr. President.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The witness can retire. Perhaps we had better adjourn now. [A recess was taken.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: With reference to the application on behalf of the Defendant Bormann the Tribunal allows witness Number 1, Miss Else Kruger. The Tribunal allows witnesses Numbers 3 and 4, Dr. Klopfer and Helmuth Friedrich. The Tribunal also allows the witness whose name I have got inserted instead of Number 2, Christians, I think it was. With reference to the documents applied for, Numbers 1 to 7, the application is refused. But the Tribunal will consider any application for documents which the defendants' counsel, who may be appointed to argue the general questions of law on behalf of all the defendants, may wish to have translated. Document Number 11 may be translated. Counsel for the Defendant Bormann may see the documents which are mentioned under Roman Number III in the application and counsel for the Defendant Bormann may also use the documents contained under heading "B." The final decision upon the admissibility of all these documents is, of course, a matter which will be decided at the time the documents are presented. There is one other thing that I want to announce, and it is in answer to the application of Dr. Servatius on behalf of the Defendant Sauckel. I am told that the witness Timm is in Nuremberg prison. The witness Biedemann is also in Nuremberg prison. The witness Hildebrandt will probably arrive in Nuremberg today. His whereabouts had been lost and he has only just been rediscovered. The witness Jager is in the British zone, and the British secretary is trying through the military authorities to obtain his attendance. The witness Stothfang has not been located. There appears to be a mistake in the identity of the person who was reported to the General Secretary previously. The witness Mitschke has never been located, although every effort is now being made to locate him. That is all.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: I ask permission to call a further witness, Fritz Wieshofer. I shall examine this witness only very briefly, because most points have already been clarified through the other witnesses. [The witness Wieshofer took the stand.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you state your full name?

turns.speaker: FRITZ WIESHOFER (Witness)
turns.text: Fritz Wieshofer.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat this oath after me: I swear by God-the Almighty and Omniscient-that I will speak the pure truth-and will withhold and add nothing. [The witness repeated the oath.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You may sit down.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Herr Wieshofer, how old are you?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: 31 years old.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Married?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Children?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: One son.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Were you a member of the Party?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I applied for membership in 1938.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: You only applied for membership?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Were you a member of the SS or the SA?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I was in the Waffen-SS.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Since when?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Since June 1940.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Are you Austrian by birth?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I am Austrian.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: When did you join the Reich Youth Leader's Of lice?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I joined Herr Von Schirach on 3 October 1940.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: And what did you do before that?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Before that I had a temporary post in the Foreign Of lice.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: For how long?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Only from May until October 1940.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: And before that?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Before that I was employed in the Gauleiter's office in Carinthia.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did you have anything to do with the Hitler Youth? WIESHOFER: No.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: In October of 1940, then, you came to Vienna to join Von Schirach?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes, to Vienna.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: In what capacity did you go there?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I went there as Von Schirach's adjutant.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: And what did your duties mostly consist of?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: As adjutant I was responsible for the handling of the mail, engagements for conferences, seeing to it that files were presented on time at conferences, travel arrangements, and so on.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did you only work for Schirach in his capacity as Reich Governor, as Gauleiter, or did you act for him only as mayor?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I was adjutant for Herr Von Schirach in all his capacities.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did you also have access to the secret files?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Witness, I shall only have a very few brief questions to put to you. First of all, I am interested in this: Who was responsible for the forced evacuation of Jews from Vienna?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: The forced evacuation of Jews from Vienna, as far as I know, was handled by the RSHA. The representative in Vienna was a certain Dr. Brunner, an Obersturmfuehrer in the SS.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did you often visit Dr. Brunner officially in connection with the forced evacuation of Jews, and for what reason?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: In some cases, Jews who were affected by this forced evacuation made written applications to Von Schirach to be left out of the transport. In such cases, Von Schirach, through the Chief of his Central Bureau, took the matter up with Dr. Brunner's office and asked that the request of the applicant be granted. I would say that generally this was done by the Chief of the Central Bureau. I remember two cases where I myself received instructions to intervene with Dr. Brunner, not by writing or telephoning, but by going to see him personally.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: And what did this SS Sturmfuehrer Dr. Brunner tell you about what was actually going to happen to the Jews when they were taken away from Vienna?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Dr. Brunner only told me, on the occasion of one of these interventions, that the action of resettling the Jews would be a resettlement from the district of Vienna into the zone of the former Government General. He also told me in what way this was being carried out. For instance he said that women and small children would travel in second-class carriages; that sufficient rations for the journey and milk for small children would be provided. He also told me that these resettled persons, upon arrival at their destination, insofar as they were capable of working, would immediately be put to work. First of all, they would be put into assembly camps, but that as soon as accommodation was available, they would be given homes, et cetera. He also told me that because of the numerous interventions by Herr Von Schirach his work had been made very difficult.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did you, or have you-I will put my next question this way: Did you ever see an order in which Gauleiter were forbidden to intervene in any way on behalf of Jews, and did you discuss that order with Von Schirach?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I recollect a written order which we received either at the end of 1940 or at the beginning of 1941. It stated that "There are reasons which make it necessary once more to point out," et cetera. It obviously was a repetition of an order which had already been given. The purport of the order was that because of certain reasons, Gauleiter were prohibited from intervening on behalf of Jews in the future.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did you talk about that with Schirach?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I talked to Herr Von Schirach about it.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: What did he say?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: As far as I can recollect, Von Schirach wrote on the order "To be filed." He did not say anything more about it.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: I have another question, Witness. The Defendant Von Schirach was once in the concentration camp at Mauthausen. Can you tell us when that was?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I cannot tell you that exactly. All I can say on that subject is that when I came back from the front-and this was either in the autumn of 1942 or in June 1943-the adjutant who was on duty at the time told me that he had accompanied Herr Von Schirach to a concentration camp, Mauthausen Camp. Some time afterwards-it must have been when I came back from the front the second time, at the end of 1943-Herr Von Schirach also told me that he had been to Mauthausen. I only recollect that he said that he had heard a symphony concert there.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Well, we are not interested in that; we have heard that. I am only interested in one thing: Did he visit Mauthausen or another concentration camp again later on? Can you give us reliable information on that or not?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I can give you reliable information on that. That is quite out of the question, because from November 1943 until the collapse I was continuously on duty and I knew where Von Schirach was, day and night.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Did he go to Mauthausen again in 1944?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: No, certainly not, that is out of the question.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Witness, you remember that toward the end of the war there were orders coming from some source or other stating that enemy airmen who had been forced to land were no longer to be protected. Do you know of that?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: That somewhere such orders were issued?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: What was the attitude of Defendant Von Schirach regarding such orders, and how do you know about it?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I talked about these orders with Herr Von Schirach. Von Schirach was always against the idea contained in the order, and he always said that these airmen, too, should be treated as prisoners of war. Once he said: "If we do not do that, then there is the danger that our enemies, too, will treat their prisoners, that is Germans, in the same manner."

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Do you yourself know of cases where Defendant Von Schirach actuary intervened on behalf of enemy airmen in that way?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Will you please tell us about it?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: During one of the last air attacks on Vienna, in March 1945, an American plane was shot down and crashed near the headquarters of the Gau command post. That command post was on a wooded hill in Vienna to which part of the population used to go during air attacks. Von Schirach was watching from a 32-meter high iron structure on which he would always stand during air attacks, and he observed that a member of the American crew bailed out of the aircraft. He immediately ordered the commander in charge of this command post to drive to the place of the landing so as to protect the American soldier against the crowd and bring him to safety. The American soldier was brought to the command post and after the air attack he was handed over to the Air Force Command XVII as a prisoner of war.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: When did you leave Vienna?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I left Vienna with Herr Von Schirach on 13 April 1945.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: On 13 April together with the Defendant Von Schirach?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Together with Herr Von Schirach.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Now, this is the last question I have to put to you: Witness, have you ever heard from Schirach's lips anything to the effect that Henna was to be held "to the last man" at all costs, or that destruction should be carried out in Vienna?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I have never heard him say either the one or the other.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Mr. President, I have no further questions to put this witness.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Witness, do you know the Prater in Vienna?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes, of course, I am Viennese.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: What sort of an institution is that?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: The Prater is, or at least was, a pleasure park.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Was it closed during the war?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: The Prater was not closed during the war.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: What sort of people used to go there?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: During the war you mean?

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Workers, employees, civil servants, that is the Viennese, the whole of Vienna.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Did you also see foreign workers there?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: A great many or just a few?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: The situation in Vienna was such that we used to say that if you wanted to go to the Prater then you would have to be able to speak French and Russian, because with Viennese alone you could not get along. The Prater was overcrowded with foreign workers.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: How were these foreigners dressed, badly or well?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: These foreigners were well dressed, so that you could not distinguish them from the population. Only when they talked could you recognize that they were foreigners.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: How did they look otherwise? As regards food, did they look starved?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: As far as I myself could see, the workers looked perfectly well fed.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Did the people have money?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: They had lots of money. It was known that the "black market" in Vienna was almost entirely dominated by foreign workers.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Could foreigners be seen only in the Prater or were they to be seen everywhere in the town?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Not only in the Prater, but also in the rest of the town, in cafes, of which there are so many in Vienna, in restaurants, and in hotels.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: I have no further questions.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Whom, besides the Defendant Von Schirach, do you know of these defendants? And by "know" I mean know personally, or have some acquaintanceship with the person, or had something to do with the person?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Personally, I only know Herr Funk.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Do you know Sauckel?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Wed, who else?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I know Herr Seyss-Inquart, but I did not have any personal dealings with him. I was the adjutant of Von Schirach.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: How do you know Funk?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I was invited by Herr Funk a few times. Officially, as adjutant of Herr Von Schirach, I had some dealings with hint, and apart from that, he invited me several times privately.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Were you in the SS at that time, when you were invited by Funk?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: At that time I was in the Waffen-SS as an officer.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: By the way, when did you first join the SS?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I joined the Waffen-SS on 26 June 1940.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Were you in any other branch of the SS besides the Waffen-SS?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I was also in the General SS.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: When did you join the General SS?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: In June or July 1939.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: So you were actually in the SS from as far back as 1939?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: In the General SS; yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Now, you also became an SS Obersturmfuehrer at one time, did you not?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: When was that?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I became Obersturmfuehrer about 21 June 1944.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: When did you join the SA?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I joined the SA on 9 May 1932.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Did you know the Strasshof Camp, S-t-r-a-s-s-h-o-f?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: This is the first time I have heard that name.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Wen, it may have been mispronounced. It was a camp located outside Vienna.

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I do not know which camp you mean. I understood Strasshof. I do not know of any such camp.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Yes, something like that. You never heard of that camp?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Never.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: And you were in Vienna from what year?-19 . . .?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I was born in Vienna.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Wen, I know you were, but I am talking about your service with the Defendant Schirach. You were there with him for how long?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: From the beginning of October 1940.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: And you never heard of Strasshof?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Did you have much to do with the files of this Defendant Von Schirach?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: What would you say you had to do with them? What was your responsibility?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I merely had to see to it that files were presented in good time for the conference, and that after they had been used they were returned to the Central Bureau.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Where would you go to get a file for Von Schirach that had to do with the Reich Defense Commission for that district or that defense district? Where would you go to get a file that had to do with matters concerning the Reich Defense Commission? Now, let us assume a situation-let me make it clear to you. Say that Von Schirach tells you he wants a file about a certain matter that has to do with the Reich Defense Commission. You had to have it on his desk by a certain hour and see that it was there, as you say. Tell the Tribunal just what you would do, where you would go, who you would talk to, and how you would get that for him.

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: That would be simple for me. I would apply to the Chief of the Central Bureau, knowing that he would probably have to go to the Regierungsprasident to obtain that file. That is what I assume. I myself would only have gone to the Central Bureau.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: You had a central filing place, did you not, for all of your files, whether they were under the Reich Defense Commission or the Gauleiter or the civil government of Vienna; is that not so? They were all kept in one place?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: They were not all together in one place; only a part of the files were in the Central Bureau. I cannot tell you which part because I have never had anything to do with that.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: You left Vienna on 13 April, you say, with Von Schirach?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: I suppose, as his adjutant, you had to make considerable preparations for leaving for some days previously, did you not?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: What did you pack up? What did you take with you?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: We did not take anything with us from Vienna. Von Schirach went by car, and the gentlemen on his staff went in two or three other cars. Nothing else was taken along from Vienna.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Well, what did you do in the of lice; how did you leave it?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: We had not used the office since, I think, the spring or early summer of 1944, because the "Ballhausplatz," that is, the of lice of the Reich Governor, had a direct hit and Von Schirach could no longer work there. He was working in his apartment.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: In his apartment? And did he have an his files in his apartment or somewhere near at hand?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: He had no files whatever in his apartment. They remained in the office, in that part of the Reich Governor's building which was still being used and in which one could still work.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Were any files taken out of the filing department of the Reich Governor's Office when you left Vienna, or before you left Vienna?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I do not know anything about that. I know that an order existed, both for the State Administration as well as for the Party, that files must be destroyed when the enemy approached. Whether that was done or what actually happened to the files, I do not know.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Who got that order?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: The order, as far as the Party channels were concerned, went to the deputy Gauleiter, and as far as the State Administration was concerned, to the Regierungsprasident.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Did you also receive an order to start moving your files to places of safety some time in the spring of 1945 or even the late winter of 1944?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I have no recollection of such an order.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Do you know that some 250 folders of your files were moved to a salt mine outside Henna? Do you know anything about that?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: No, I hear that for the first time.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Do you know that there is such a mine near Vienna? You have lived there quite a while, I gather.

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: No. It is not near Vienna-if I may be permitted to put this matter right-but near Salzburg; we never lived there. I only know that this mine exists.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: How far is it from Vienna?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Approximately 350 kilometers.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: You do not know anything about any files being taken there. You are sure about that, are you?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I am absolutely certain; I do not know anything about that.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: I have just one other question to ask. I suppose you knew the defendant pretty well. He is a little older than you, but you had worked for him for some time. Is that not so?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Why did you not join the Army instead of the SS when you wanted to do something for your country?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: When I was called up, the Waffen SS was considered the elite unit and I preferred to serve in such a guards unit, if I may say so, than in the general Armed Forces.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Was it partly due to the fact that you had been in the General SS since 1939?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: No. That had nothing to do with it. Many members of the General SS went to the Forces.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Did you talk this matter over with your superior, the Youth Leader Von Schirach, before you joined the SS in 1939, and the Waffen SS later on?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: No. Might I remind you that I did not join Von Schirach until October 1940, whereas I joined the Waffen SS on 26 June 1940.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Yes, but you were, I suppose, a young man and you were in touch with the Reich Youth organization in 1939 when you joined the General SS. Is that not a fact? Were you not a part of the Youth organization in 1939?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: No. I was not taken into the Youth Officers Corps until April 1944 when I became Bannfuehrer. Before that I had nothing to do with it.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Well, I do not think you understand me. It is not too important, but how old were you in 1939? You were 24, approximately, were you not?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: And were you not then in some way affiliated with the Hitler Youth or the Youth organization in Germany, either as a member, or having something to do with it?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: No. Neither as a member nor in any other way. Of course I knew Youth Leaders in Carinthia, yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: You were quite a speech maker for the Party, were you not, during your lifetime?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I spoke at several meetings in Carinthia between April 1938 and May 1940.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: At about how many meetings would you say you spoke in that period of 2 years?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: During that time I spoke at about 80 meetings.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Before an average of about, say, 3,000 persons per meeting?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I also spoke in very small villages. I would say that the average attendance would be about 200.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: That is all I have.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Do you want to re examine?

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: What were the subjects you talked about at these meetings?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Our subject was given to us by the Reich Propaganda Ministry. The meetings were conducted in such a way that every speaker was able to talk on general matters. For instance the subject might have been "With the Fuehrer to Final Victory," or "Why Welfare for the Nation?" or "Why Winter Relief?" Such subjects were always given.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Did you read Rosenberg's Myth of the 20th Century ?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Did you speak about such subjects?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Never; in view of my education I would not have been in a position to do so.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Have you ever read this Myth?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I have not read the Myth.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Did you speak to youth at these meetings?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I did not speak to youth-that is, not particularly to youth.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Mr. President, I do not wish to put any questions to the witness; thank you very much.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Witness, did Schirach have any authority to intervene in case of Jews who were being deported from Henna?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: He had no authority to do so, but he did it.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: How many times did he intervene?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I cannot recollect a single case where Von Schirach did not intervene when he received a petition.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: I did not ask that; I asked how many times he intervened.

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I cannot give you any figure without being inaccurate. It is difficult to say.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Did he intervene many times, or a few?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: No. He intervened often.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (MR. Biddle)
turns.text: Did you see the order to the Police not to protect aviators? You said it was in writing, did you not?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Who signed it?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: The order was signed by Bormann.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: And was it distributed to the Police in Vienna?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: By the Police? If I have understood you rightly, you were talking about the order that Gauleiter must not intervene on behalf of Jews.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: No. This was the order about not protecting aviators who had crashed. You said you saw that order, did you not?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I did see the order, yes. I can no longer remember whom it came from and to whom, it was addressed. It was merely sent to our office for our information. We were not called upon to take any action.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Do you not know whether or not the Police had a copy of it?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Please, will you be good enough to repeat the question?

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Do you know whether or not the Police in Vienna had copies of the order?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: That I do not know.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Did you ever know Himmler?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I have seen him.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Did he give you any instructions?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Did you get any instructions from the SS?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: In which way do you mean?

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Any instructions from the SS directly when you were in Von Schirach's of lice?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: No.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: None at all?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: None at all. I cannot recollect any.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: I think you said once that Schirach sent a command to save American aviators from the crowd, did you not? Do you not understand?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes, I understand, and I did say that.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: And what other efforts did Von Schirach make to protect aviators from the crowd? Did he make any other efforts?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Did he issue any orders to the Police or take it up with the Police?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Von Schirach's opinion was known. In the circles . . .

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: I did not ask you the opinion. Did he issue any orders to the Police or talk to the Police?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I have no recollection of that.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Well, you would know if he had, would you not?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: If I had been present when he gave the orders then I would know it, but it is possible that he talked when I was not there.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Did you say you had access to the secret files?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: What was kept in the secret files?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: I did not understand the question.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: I asked you what was kept, what was put in the secret files, what sort of papers?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: There were secret files which came from the Supreme Party Headquarters, secret files which came from the Minister of the Interior; there were things which made one wonder why they were called "secret." But as far as details of these files are concerned, I cannot, of course, today remember them.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: And I suppose any documents, any reports, that were marked "secret" would be put in those secret files, would they not?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: Reports from us to higher departments, or do you mean from the top downwards?

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: Reports coming in to you.

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: They would then have been filed in the secret archives.

turns.speaker: THE TRIBUNAL (Mr. Biddle)
turns.text: And SS secret reports would go in the secret files, would they not?

turns.speaker: WIESHOFER
turns.text: SS reports did not come to us, because we were not a service department of the SS.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: If you have no questions yourself, Dr. Sauter, then the witness may retire.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: No. [The witness Wieshofer left the stand.]

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Mr. President, in Schirach's document book there are still a few documents which, up to now, have not been expressly presented; but I believe it is not necessary to read these documents to you. To save time, I should like, if I may, to refer to the documents and ask you to take judicial notice of them; for instance, of the affidavit of Frau Hoepken, which is incorporated in the document book under Number 3 and which has already been submitted somewhere else. There is only one document, Mr. President, about which I want to give a very brief explanation. In the Schirach document book, under Number 118(a), there is the farewell letter of the explorer Dr. Colin Ross. With reference to this Dr. Colin Ross, when the documents were dealt with, the Prosecutor said that the body of Dr. Ross had not been discovered. My first reaction was of course surprise, and I made inquiries as to what actually had been done with these bodies and I discovered that in fact on 30 April 1945, the day before the arrival of American troops, the bodies of Dr. Colin Ross and his wife were found in the house of Defendant Von Schirach at Urfeld, on Lake Walchen. They had both first taken poison and then, to be quite sure, Or. Ross shot his wife and then himself. German soldiers who were still at Urfeld on Lake Walchen as patients at the time then buried the bodies quite close to the house of the Defendant Von Schirach. In the autumn the American Governor ordered that the bodies were to be transferred to the cemetery, but eventually he rescinded that order and permitted the bodies to remain where they had originally been buried.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Sauter, can you indicate in what way you will submit this document has any relevance at all? We have read the document. N does not appear to have any striking relevance.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Mr. President, we have submitted this document because it is to prove, or at least indicate, that the Defendant Von Schirach, together with this Dr. Colin Ross, continuously worked to maintain peace, and later on to limit the war. Therefore it is submitted only to show that the Defendant Von Schirach worked for peace.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: The document does not mention Von Schirach or in any way indicate that he had worked for peace.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: But it says in the document, "We have done every thing in our power to prevent this war, or. .."

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Sauter, the word "We" must mean the people who "leave this world by our own will," namely Dr. Colin Ross and his wife. It does not refer to Von Schirach.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: We do not know that. Why should it not also refer to Von Schirach?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Well, because there is such a thing as grammar. The document begins "We leave this world by our own will."

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: As to that, Mr. President, may I remind you that this name, Dr. Colin Ross, has been mentioned very often during this trial in connection with the peace efforts of the Defendant Von Schirach, and that Dr. Colin Ross, together with his wife, was living in Schirach's apartment when they committed suicide.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Well, very well, Dr. Sauter, if you wish to draw our attention to it, you may do so.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Thank you. Mr. President, this letter was not really meant for the public; the original of the letter was left behind by Dr. Ross, and a number of carbon copies were sent to personal friends. In this way we found this letter of Dr. Colin Ross. I do not think there is anything else I have to say.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I have not said anything critical of the letter. If you want to read some sentences of it, read them; if you do not we will take judicial notice of it. As I tell you, we have already read this letter.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: I am not stopping your reading a sentence of it, if you want to read a sentence of it.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: It is of course not necessary, Mr. President, if you have taken cognizance of it. I have nothing else to say, and at this point I can end my case for the Defendant Von Schirach.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Sauter, have you offered in evidence all the documents which are in these books?

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Then they will be numbered with the numbers which are in the books.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well, then we will take judicial notice of them all.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Well, Mr. President, there is one here which the Tribunal expressly ruled on-the affidavit of Uiberreither. The Defendant Von Schirach was told he would have to present Uiberreither if he were to use this affidavit. He has not been presented here and now the affidavit is being offered. We expressly asked that he be called here if this affidavit was to be submitted to the Tribunal.

turns.speaker: DR. SAUTER
turns.text: I am not making any reference to Uiberreither's affidavit, and I will forego calling the witness Uiberreither.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Very well, Dr. Sauter.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Then the affidavit is not offered?

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: No, it is not being offered.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: That is Page 135.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Then it will not be admitted, and we will adjourn now. The Tribunal recessed until 1400 hours.] Afternoon Sessions

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Mr. President, during the presentation of the case involving the Defendant Funk, there was a number of documents that we did not submit in evidence at the time; and I asked the Tribunal's permission to do so at a later time. I am prepared to do so now if the Tribunal would care to have me.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes, I think it would be quite convenient now.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Very well, Sir. The first one is a matter of clarifying the record with respect to it. It is Document 2828 PS. It has already been offered in evidence as Exhibit USA 654. But the excerpt, or the extract, which was read will be found on Page 105 of the document. We cited another page, which was in error. Reference to this Document USA 654 will be found on Page 9071 (Volume XIII, Page 141) of the record. We also offered our Document EC 440, which consisted of a statement made by the Defendant Funk, and we quoted a sentence from Page 4 of that document. I wish to offer that as Exhibit USA 874. Then Document 3952 PS was an interrogation of the Defendant Funk dated 19 October 1945. We wish to offer that as USA 875. I might remind the Tribunal that the excerpt quoted from that interrogation had to do with the statement made by Funk that the Defendant Hess had notified him of the impending attack on the Soviet Union. That excerpt has been translated into the four languages, and therefore will be readily available to the Tribunal Then there is also another interrogation dated 22 October 1945. We read from Pages 15 and 16 of that interrogation, as it appears in the record at Page 9169 for 7 May (Volume XIII, Page 214). The document is Number 3953 PS; we offer it as Exhibit USA 876. We next referred to Document Number 3894 PS, the interrogation of one Hans Posse. We offered it as Exhibit USA 843 at the time, as appears on Page 9093 of the record for 6 May (Volume xii, Page 158). At that time I stated to the Tribunal that we would submit the whole interrogation in French, Russian, German, and English. We are now prepared to do that, and do so. Then we have Document 3954 PS. This is an affidavit by one Franz B. Wolf, one of the editors of the Frankfurter Zeitung. Reference to it will be found at Page 9082 of the transcript, where we stated that we would have more to say about the reason for the retention of the editorial staff of the Frankfurter Zeitung (Volume XIII, Page 150). That Document, 3954-PS, is also now available to the Tribunal in French, Russian, German, and English; and we offer it as Exhibit USA 877. Then, Mr. President, a motion picture film was shown during this cross examination of the Defendant Funk; and the Tribunal inquired as to whether or not we would be prepared to submit affidavits giving its source, and so on. We are now prepared to do so; and we offer first an affidavit by Captain Sam Harris who arranged to have the pictures taken, which becomes Exhibit USA 878. The second affidavit is by the photographer who actually took the picture. We offer that as Exhibit USA 879. Finally, I should also like to clear up one other matter. On March 25, during the cross examination of the witness Bohle, witness for the Defendant Hess, Colonel Amen quoted from the interrogation of Von Strempel, as appears in the record beginning at Page 6482 (Volume X, Page 40). We have had the pertinent portions translated into the operating languages of the Tribunal, and we ask that this interrogation, which bears our Document Number 3800 PS, be admitted in evidence as Exhibit USA 880. I believe, Mr. President, that clears up all of the documents that we have not offered formally, up to this date.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Now, counsel for the Defendant Sauckel.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: With the permission of the Tribunal, I Will now call Defendant Sauckel to the witness stand. Am; PRESIDENT: Certainly. The Defendant Sauckel took the stand.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you state your full name?

turns.speaker: FRITZ SAUCKEL (Defendant)
turns.text: Ernst Friedrich Christoph Sauckel.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Will you repeat this oath after me: I swear by God-the Almighty and Omniscient-that I will speak the pure truth-and will withhold and add nothing. [The defendant repeated the oath.]

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: You may sit down.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Witness, please describe your career to the Tribunal.

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I was the only child of the postman Friedrich Sauckel, and was born at Hassfurt on the Main near Bamberg. I attended the elementary school at Schweinfurt and the secondary school.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Mow long were you at the secondary school?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: For 5 years. As my father held only a very humble position, it was my mother, a seamstress, who made it possible for me to go to that school. When she became very ill with heart trouble, I saw that it would be impossible for my parents to provide for my studies, and I obtained their permission to go to sea to make a career for myself there.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Did you join the merchant marine, or where did you go?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: First of all I joined the Norwegian and Swedish merchant marine so that I could be thoroughly trained in seaman ship on the big sailing vessels and clippers.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: How old were you at the time?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: At that time I was 15 1/2.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: What were you earning?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: As a cabin boy on a Norwegian sailing ship I earned 5 kronen in addition to my keep.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: And then, in the course of your career at sea, where did you go next?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: In the course of my career as a sailor, and during my training which I continued afterwards on German sailing vessels, I sailed on every sea and went to every part of the world.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Did you come into contact with foreign families?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: Through the Young Men's Christian Association, principally in Australia and North America, as well as in South America, I came into contact with families of these countries.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Where were you when the first World War started?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: It so happened that I was on a German sailing vessel on the way to Australia when the ship was captured, and on the high seas I was made prisoner by the French.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: How long did you remain prisoner?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: Five years, until November 1919.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: And did you return home then?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: Yes, I returned home then.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: And then what did you do?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: Although I had finished my trading and studies in seamanship required of me, I could not go to sea again and take my examination, since my savings made during those years at sea had become worthless because of the German inflation. There were also few German ships and very many unemployed German seamen, so I decided to take up work in a factory in my home town of Schweinfurt.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Did you remain in your home town?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: At first I remained in my home town. I learned to be a turner and engineer in the Fischer ball bearing factory in order to save money so that I later could attend a technical school, an engineering college.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Were you already interested in politics at that time?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: Although as a sailor I despised politics-for I loved my sailor's life and still love it today-conditions forced me to take up a definite attitude towards, political problems. No one in Germany at that time could do otherwise. Many years before I had left a beautiful country and a rich nation and I returned to that country 6 years later to find it fundamentally changed and in a state of upheaval, and in great spiritual and material need.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Did you join any party?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: No. I worked in a factory which people in my home town described as "ultra Red." I worked in the tool shop, and right and left of me Social Democrats, Communists, Socialists, and Anarchists were working-among others my present father law- and during all the rest periods discussions went on, so that whether one wanted to or not one became involved in the social problems of the time.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: You mention your father-in-law. Did you marry then?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: In 1923 I married the daughter of a German workman I had met at that time. I am still happily married to her today and we have 10 children.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: When did you join the Party?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I joined the Party definitely in 1923 after having already been in sympathy with it before.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: What made you do it?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: One of those days I heard a speech of Hitler's. In this speech he said that the German factory worker and the German laborer must make common cause with the German brain worker. The controversies between the proletariat and the middle class must be smoothed out and bridged over by each getting to know and understand the other. Through this a new community of people would grow up, and only such a community, not bound to middle class or proletariat, could overcome the dire needs of those days and the splitting up of the German nation into parties and creeds. This statement took such hold of me and struck me so forcibly, that I dedicated my life to the idea of adjusting what seemed to be almost irreconcilable contrasts. I did that all the more, if I may say so, because I was aware of the fact that there is an inclination to go to extremes in German people, and in the German character generally. I had to examine myself very thoroughly to find the right path for me personally. As I have already said, I had hardly taken any interest in political questions. My good parents, who are no longer alive, brought me up in a strictly Christian but also in a very patriotic way. However, when I went to sea, I lived a sailor's life. I loaded saltpeter in Chile. I did heavy lumber work in Canada, in Quebec. I trimmed coal on the equator, and I sailed around Cape Horn several times. All of this was hard work; I ask...

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Please, come back to the question of the Party.

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: This has to do with the question of the Party, for we must an give some reasons as to how we got there. I myself ...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Dr. Servatius, I stated at the beginning of the defendant's case that we had heard this account from the Defendant Goering and that we did not propose to hear it again from 20 defendants. It seems to me that we are having it inflicted upon us by nearly every one of the defendants.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: I believe, Mr. President, that we are interested in getting some sort of an impression of the defendant himself. Seen from various points of view, the facts look different. I will now briefly...

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: It is quite true, Dr. Servatius, but we have had half an hour, almost, of it now.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: I shall limit it now. The Party was dissolved in 1923, and refounded in 1925. Did you join it again?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Did you take an active part in the Party or were you just a member?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: From 1925 on I took an active part in it.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: And what position did you hold?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I was then Gauleiter in Thuringia.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Did you do that to get work, to earn your living, or for what reason?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: As Gauleiter in Thuringia I earned 150 marks. In any other profession I would have had accommodations and earned more money.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: When did you make Hitler's acquaintance? SAUCKEL: I met him briefly in 1925.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: When did you become Gauleiter?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I became Gauleiter in 1927.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: And how were you appointed?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I was appointed by letter.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Did you receive any special instructions which pointed to secret intentions of the Party?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: At that time we were very definitely told that under no circumstances should there be any secret chapters or any other secrecy in the life of the Party, but that everything should be done publicly.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Who was your predecessor?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: Dr. Dinter.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Why was he relieved of his post?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: Dr. Dinter was dismissed because he wanted to found a new religious movement within the Party.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: In 1929 you became a member of the Thuringian Diet?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Were you elected to that?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I was elected to the Diet in the same way as at every parliamentary election.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Was dictatorship in power there already at the time?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: That was not possible; the province was governed in accordance with the Thuringian constitution.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: How long were you a member of the Diet?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I was a member of the Diet as long as it existed, until May 1933.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: How was it dissolved?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: The Diet was dissolved by a Reich Government decree.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Then in 1932, you were a member of the Provincial Government of Thuringia. How did you get into that position?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: In 1932, in the month of June, new elections took place for the Thuringian Diet, and the NSDAP obtained 26 out of 60 seats.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Was any mention made of a dictatorship which was to be aimed at?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: No, a government was elected according to parliamentary principles. '

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Wed, you had a majority in the Thuringian Government, had you not, and you could use your influence?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: Together with the bourgeois parties, by an absolute majority, a National Socialist government was elected.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: What happened to the old officials? Were they dismissed?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I myself became the President and Minister of the Interior in that government; the old officials, without exception, remained in their offices.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: And with what did that first National Socialist government concern itself in the field of domestic politics?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: In the field of domestic politics there was only one question at that time, and that was the alleviation of an indescribable distress which is only exceeded by that of today.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: In this connection, Mr. President, may I submit two government reports from which I only wish to draw your attention briefly to two passages. One is the report contained in Document Number 96, which shows the activity of the government and its fight against social distress. What is particularly important when you run through it, is what is not mentioned, that is, there is no mention of the question of war or other such matters, but again and again the alleviation of distress is mentioned. And important, too, is the work that was carried out. That is in Document Number 97. In this book, on Page 45, there is a statement of the work undertaken by the government-bridge building, road making, and so on-and in no way had this work anything to do with war. Then I am submitting Document Number 95 from the same period. It is a book called Sauckel's Fighting Speeches. Here, too, the book is remarkable for what does not appear in it, namely preparations for war. Instead it emphasizes the distress which must be alleviated. It becomes clear from the Individual articles that these are speeches made during a number of years, which show in a similar way what the preoccupations were of the Defendant Sauckel It begins in 1932 with a speech dealing with the misery of the time, and ends with the final questions where reference is made once again to the alleviation of social need and the preservation of peace. The Tribunal will be able to read these articles in the document book. In 1933 you also became Reich Regent of Thuringia. How did you manage to get to that position?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I was appointed Reich Regent of Thuringia by Field Marshal Von Hindenburg, who was Reich President at that time.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: What were the instructions you received when you took up your offices?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: When I took over my office as Reich Regent I received instructions to form a new Thuringian Government, as the Reich Regent was to keep out of the administrative affairs of a German state...

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: You need not tell us these technical details. I mean what political task were you given?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I was given the political task of administering Thuringia as Reich Regent within the existing Reich law and prevailing Constitution, and of guaranteeing the unity of the Reich.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: And did the words "guarantee the unity of the Reich" mean the overpowering of others, in particular the authorities in Thuringia?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: No, the authorities remained.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Now, you held both the position of Gauleiter and that of Reich Regent. What was the aim of that?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: Both positions were entirely separate in their organizations. Under the Regent were officials in office, and under the Gauleiter were employees of the Party. Both positions were administered absolutely separately, as is the case in any other state where members of a party are at the same time party officials or leaders and exercise both these functions simultaneously.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: So you received no order that one position should absorb the other?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: No, I had no such orders. The tasks were entirely different.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Were you a member of the SA?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I myself was never an SA man. I was an honorary Obergruppenfuehrer in the SA.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: How did you receive that appointment?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I cannot tell you. It was honorary.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Were you appointed SS Obergruppenfuehrer by Himmler?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: No, the Fuehrer made me honorary SS Obergruppenfuehrer for no special reason and without functions.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Were you a member of the Reichstag?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: Yes, from 1933 on.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: As a member of the Reichstag, did you know anything in advance about the beginning of the war? Were you informed?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I was never informed in advance about the start of the war or about foreign political developments. I merely remember that quite suddenly-it may have been during the days between 24 August and the end of August-we were called to a session of the Reichstag in Berlin. This session was canceled at the time, and we were later ordered to go to the Fuehrer, that is, the Gauleiter and Reichsleiter. But a number had already left so that the circle was not complete. The conference, or Hitler's speech, only lasted a short time. He said, roughly, that the meeting of the Reichstag could not take place as things were still in the course of development. He was convinced that there would not be a war. He said he hoped there would be some settlement in a small way and meant by that, as I had to conclude, a solution without the parts of Upper Silesia lost in 1921. He said-and that I remember exactly-that Danzig would become German, and apart from that Germany would be given a railway line with several tracks, like a Reichsautobahn, with a strip of ground to the right and left of it. He told us to go home and prepare for the Reich Party Rally, where we would meet again.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: Did you have any close connections with the Fuehrer?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
turns.text: I personally, as far as I know the Fuehrer, had a great deal of admiration for him. But I had no close connection with him that one could describe as personal. I had a number of discussions with him about the administration of my Gau and in particular about the care he wished to be given to cultural buildings in Thuringia-in Weimar, Eisenach, and Meiningen; and later on there were more frequent meetings because of my position as Plenipotentiary General for the Allocation of Labor.

turns.speaker: DR. SERVATIUS
turns.text: We shall come to that later. What connections did you have with the Reichsleiter?

turns.speaker: SAUCKEL
